primitives.name: U(1) Symmetry Group
primitives.formalStatement: U(1) is the group of complex numbers of unit modulus under multiplication: U(1) = {e^{i*theta} : theta in [0, 2*pi)}. It is the gauge group of electromagnetism, with phase rotations psi -> e^{i*alpha} psi leaving the Lagrangian invariant.

dependencies.description: U(1) is a Lie group
U(1) is the unit circle in the complex plane

compositionRules.yields: Electroweak gauge group SU(2) x U(1)
conditions: Combined symmetry before spontaneous symmetry breaking
compositionRules.example: The Standard Model electroweak sector has gauge group SU(2)_L x U(1)_Y, broken to U(1)_EM by the Higgs mechanism

applicabilityPatterns: Describing electromagnetic gauge invariance
Modeling phase symmetries in quantum mechanics
Classifying abelian gauge theories

primitives.name: SU(2) Symmetry Group
primitives.formalStatement: SU(2) is the group of 2x2 unitary matrices with determinant 1. It is the gauge group of the weak nuclear force and is locally isomorphic to SO(3), the rotation group in 3D. Its Lie algebra su(2) has basis {sigma_1/2, sigma_2/2, sigma_3/2} (Pauli matrices).

dependencies.description: SU(2) is a Lie group
SU(2) elements are linear transformations

compositionRules.yields: Non-abelian gauge group of the Standard Model: SU(3) x SU(2) x U(1)
conditions: All three gauge groups act independently
compositionRules.example: The complete Standard Model gauge group SU(3)_C x SU(2)_L x U(1)_Y describes strong, weak, and electromagnetic interactions

applicabilityPatterns: Describing weak nuclear force gauge symmetry
Modeling isospin and weak interactions
Representing 3D rotations via double cover

primitives.name: SU(3) Symmetry Group
primitives.formalStatement: SU(3) is the group of 3x3 unitary matrices with determinant 1. It is the gauge group of quantum chromodynamics (QCD), governing the strong nuclear force. Its 8 generators correspond to 8 gluons, the force carriers of the strong interaction.

dependencies.description: SU(3) is a Lie group
SU(3) elements are linear transformations in color space

compositionRules.yields: QCD equations of motion for quark fields
compositionRules.example: The QCD Lagrangian includes quark fields transforming under SU(3) with gluon fields as gauge connections

applicabilityPatterns: Describing strong nuclear force interactions between quarks
Classifying hadrons using color charge representations
Modeling gluon self-interactions

primitives.name: Gauge Principle
primitives.formalStatement: The gauge principle states that physics must be invariant under local (spacetime-dependent) symmetry transformations. Requiring local gauge invariance necessitates the introduction of gauge fields (connections) that transform as A_mu -> g A_mu g^{-1} + (i/e) g partial_mu g^{-1}.
primitives.computationalForm: Replace ordinary derivatives with gauge-covariant derivatives: D_mu = partial_mu - i g A_mu. The gauge field A_mu transforms to compensate for the spacetime-dependent phase, ensuring the Lagrangian remains invariant.

dependencies.description: Gauge groups provide the symmetry structure
Gauge fields live in the Lie algebra (a vector space)
Gauge-covariant derivatives modify differential equations

compositionRules.yields: Construction of gauge theories from any Lie group
conditions: Matter fields transform under a representation
compositionRules.example: Starting with SU(2) symmetry and applying the gauge principle generates the W and Z bosons as gauge fields

applicabilityPatterns: Constructing quantum field theories from symmetry requirements
Deriving force-carrying particles from local invariance
Understanding why fundamental forces have the structure they do

primitives.name: Lagrangian Formulation
primitives.formalStatement: The Lagrangian density L encodes the dynamics of a field theory. The action S = integral L d^4x is stationary under field variations (Hamilton's principle), yielding the Euler-Lagrange field equations. The Standard Model Lagrangian L_SM = L_gauge + L_fermion + L_Higgs + L_Yukawa.
primitives.computationalForm: Write L as a function of fields phi and their derivatives partial_mu phi. Apply the Euler-Lagrange equation: partial_mu (partial L / partial(partial_mu phi)) - partial L / partial phi = 0. For the Standard Model, this generates coupled field equations for all particles.

dependencies.description: Lagrangian mechanics uses variational principles (extremizing the action)
The action is an integral of the Lagrangian
Euler-Lagrange equations are differential equations of motion

compositionRules.yields: Gauge-invariant Lagrangian with gauge field kinetic terms
compositionRules.example: QED Lagrangian: L = psi_bar(i gamma^mu D_mu - m)psi - (1/4)F_{mu nu}F^{mu nu}

applicabilityPatterns: Formulating physical theories from symmetry principles
Deriving equations of motion for fields and particles
Encoding all interactions in a single mathematical object

primitives.name: Noether's Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: For every continuous symmetry of the action, there exists a corresponding conserved quantity. If the Lagrangian is invariant under a continuous transformation phi -> phi + epsilon * delta_phi, then the current j^mu = (partial L / partial(partial_mu phi)) delta_phi is conserved: partial_mu j^mu = 0.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Identify a continuous symmetry transformation of the Lagrangian. 2. Compute the variation delta_phi. 3. Construct the Noether current j^mu. 4. The conserved charge Q = integral j^0 d^3x is constant in time: dQ/dt = 0.

dependencies.description: Noether's theorem operates on the Lagrangian/action
Symmetries form groups
Conserved charge is a spatial integral of the current

compositionRules.yields: Gauge conservation laws: charge, color charge, weak isospin
compositionRules.example: U(1) gauge symmetry -> electric charge conservation; time translation symmetry -> energy conservation

applicabilityPatterns: Deriving conservation laws from symmetry principles
Understanding the deep connection between symmetry and physics
Finding conserved quantities for novel physical theories

primitives.name: Higgs Mechanism
primitives.formalStatement: Spontaneous symmetry breaking via a scalar field phi with potential V(phi) = -mu^2 |phi|^2 + lambda |phi|^4 gives gauge bosons mass. The vacuum expectation value <phi> = v/sqrt(2) breaks SU(2) x U(1)_Y -> U(1)_EM, generating masses m_W = gv/2, m_Z = v*sqrt(g^2+g'^2)/2.
primitives.computationalForm: Write the Higgs doublet phi = (phi+, phi0)^T. Expand around the vacuum: phi0 = (v + h)/sqrt(2). Substitute into the Lagrangian. The cross terms between phi and gauge fields generate mass terms for W+/-, Z bosons. The photon remains massless.

dependencies.description: Higgs mechanism requires gauge symmetry to break
The electroweak SU(2) symmetry is broken
The Higgs field settles at the minimum of its potential

compositionRules.yields: Massive W and Z bosons with predicted mass ratios
conditions: Higgs potential has Mexican hat shape
compositionRules.example: The predicted mass ratio m_W/m_Z = cos(theta_W) ≈ 0.88 was confirmed experimentally

applicabilityPatterns: Explaining how gauge bosons acquire mass
Understanding mass generation without breaking gauge invariance at the Lagrangian level
Predicting particle masses from symmetry breaking patterns

primitives.name: Spontaneous Symmetry Breaking
primitives.formalStatement: Spontaneous symmetry breaking occurs when the ground state of a system does not share the symmetry of the Lagrangian. A potential V(phi) invariant under a symmetry group G may have minima that are only invariant under a subgroup H subset G, breaking G -> H with dim(G/H) massless Goldstone bosons.

dependencies.description: Symmetry breaking relates group G to subgroup H
Ground state is found by minimizing the potential

compositionRules.yields: Gauge boson mass generation (Higgs mechanism)
conditions: Goldstone bosons become longitudinal gauge boson modes
compositionRules.example: Mexican hat potential V = -mu^2 phi^2 + lambda phi^4 has circular minimum; choosing a particular minimum breaks rotational symmetry

applicabilityPatterns: Understanding phase transitions in condensed matter and particle physics
Explaining why physical laws may have hidden symmetries
Analyzing how ordered states emerge from symmetric interactions

primitives.name: Electroweak Unification
primitives.formalStatement: The Glashow-Weinberg-Salam theory unifies electromagnetic and weak interactions under the gauge group SU(2)_L x U(1)_Y. After spontaneous symmetry breaking via the Higgs mechanism, the four gauge bosons split into massive W+/-, Z^0 and the massless photon gamma, with mixing angle theta_W.
primitives.computationalForm: Construct the SU(2)_L x U(1)_Y gauge Lagrangian with fermion doublets and singlets. Add Higgs doublet with Mexican hat potential. After symmetry breaking: W_mu^+/- = (W_mu^1 -/+ i W_mu^2)/sqrt(2), Z_mu = cos(theta_W) W_mu^3 - sin(theta_W) B_mu, A_mu = sin(theta_W) W_mu^3 + cos(theta_W) B_mu.

dependencies.description: Electroweak theory is a gauge theory
Higgs mechanism breaks the electroweak symmetry
Weak force is SU(2)_L gauge theory
Hypercharge is U(1)_Y gauge symmetry

compositionRules.yields: The complete Standard Model: SU(3) x SU(2) x U(1)
compositionRules.example: The Standard Model combines QCD (SU(3) color) with electroweak (SU(2) x U(1)) into a single consistent quantum field theory

applicabilityPatterns: Understanding why electromagnetic and weak forces are aspects of the same interaction
Predicting W and Z boson masses from symmetry parameters
Calculating weak interaction cross-sections

primitives.name: Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD)
primitives.formalStatement: QCD is the SU(3) gauge theory of the strong nuclear force. Quarks carry color charge (red, green, blue) and interact via 8 massless gluons. Key properties: asymptotic freedom (coupling decreases at high energy), confinement (free quarks never observed), and chiral symmetry breaking.
primitives.computationalForm: The QCD Lagrangian: L = sum_q psi_bar_q(i gamma^mu D_mu - m_q)psi_q - (1/4)G^a_{mu nu}G^{a mu nu}, where D_mu = partial_mu - ig_s (lambda^a/2) A^a_mu is the covariant derivative with Gell-Mann matrices lambda^a.

dependencies.description: QCD is the SU(3) gauge theory
QCD is constructed via the gauge principle
Quantum mechanical framework for quark fields

compositionRules.yields: The complete Standard Model of particle physics
conditions: All matter content and gauge groups included
compositionRules.example: Combining SU(3)_C (QCD) with SU(2)_L x U(1)_Y (electroweak) gives the full Standard Model with 19 free parameters

applicabilityPatterns: Calculating strong interaction processes at high energies
Understanding quark confinement and hadron structure
Predicting jet production and scattering cross-sections at particle colliders

primitives.name: Feynman Diagrams
primitives.formalStatement: Feynman diagrams are graphical representations of perturbative contributions to scattering amplitudes. Each vertex represents an interaction term in the Lagrangian, internal lines are propagators, and external lines are incoming/outgoing particles. The diagram encodes an integral via the Feynman rules.
primitives.computationalForm: For each diagram: 1. Draw all topologically distinct connected diagrams with the given external lines. 2. Assign momenta to all lines. 3. Write propagator for each internal line. 4. Write vertex factor for each vertex. 5. Integrate over undetermined loop momenta. 6. Include symmetry factors.

dependencies.description: Feynman rules are derived from the Lagrangian
Each loop in a diagram contributes an integration over momentum

compositionRules.yields: Complete perturbative calculation of scattering amplitudes
conditions: Coupling constant is small enough for perturbation theory
compositionRules.example: Electron-electron scattering in QED: tree-level diagram gives Coulomb potential, one-loop diagrams give quantum corrections

applicabilityPatterns: Calculating particle scattering probabilities in quantum field theory
Organizing perturbative expansions by diagram topology
Visualizing and computing quantum interactions

primitives.name: Coupling Constants
primitives.formalStatement: Coupling constants parameterize the strength of each fundamental interaction: alpha_EM ≈ 1/137 (electromagnetic), alpha_W ≈ 1/30 (weak), alpha_s ≈ 0.12 (strong). They run with energy scale via renormalization group equations: d(alpha)/d(ln mu) = beta(alpha).
primitives.computationalForm: Solve the renormalization group equation for each coupling: alpha_i(mu) = alpha_i(mu_0) / (1 - b_i alpha_i(mu_0) ln(mu/mu_0) / (2pi)). At mu ~ 10^16 GeV, the three couplings nearly converge, suggesting grand unification.

dependencies.description: Coupling constants appear in gauge interactions
Coupling constants run due to renormalization

compositionRules.yields: Running coupling constants that predict grand unification scale
conditions: Beta functions are computed for all gauge groups
compositionRules.example: Running the three SM couplings toward high energy, they nearly meet at ~10^16 GeV, suggesting GUT unification

applicabilityPatterns: Predicting interaction strengths at different energy scales
Testing grand unification hypotheses
Computing radiative corrections to physical processes

primitives.name: Renormalization
primitives.formalStatement: Renormalization systematically absorbs ultraviolet divergences in quantum field theory into redefined (renormalized) parameters. A renormalizable theory has a finite number of divergent structures, all absorbable into a finite set of physical parameters (masses, couplings, field normalizations).
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Regularize: introduce cutoff Lambda or dimensional regularization (d = 4-epsilon). 2. Identify divergent diagrams. 3. Add counterterms to the Lagrangian to cancel divergences. 4. Express results in terms of renormalized (physical) parameters. 5. Take regulator to infinity.

dependencies.description: Divergences appear in loop diagrams
Regularization modifies loop integrals

compositionRules.yields: Finite, physically meaningful predictions from quantum field theory
compositionRules.example: QED: computing the anomalous magnetic moment of the electron to 10 decimal places via renormalized perturbation theory

applicabilityPatterns: Making quantum field theory predictions finite and physically meaningful
Understanding how physical parameters change with observation scale
Establishing which theories can make consistent predictions

primitives.name: Conservation Laws
primitives.formalStatement: Every continuous symmetry yields a conserved quantity via Noether's theorem: time translation -> energy, spatial translation -> momentum, rotation -> angular momentum, U(1) gauge -> electric charge, SU(3) color -> color charge, baryon number -> baryon conservation.
primitives.computationalForm: For each symmetry, identify the conserved Noether current j^mu and charge Q = integral j^0 d^3x. Verify dQ/dt = 0 using equations of motion. Check conservation in Feynman diagrams: conserved quantum numbers must match at each vertex.

dependencies.description: Conservation laws follow from Noether's theorem
Symmetries form groups whose structure determines what is conserved

compositionRules.yields: Complete accounting of conserved quantities in the Standard Model
compositionRules.example: Electric charge conservation: Q(p) = +1, Q(e-) = -1, so p + e- -> n + nu_e conserves total charge (0 on each side)

applicabilityPatterns: Predicting which particle reactions are allowed by conservation laws
Simplifying calculations by using conserved quantities as constraints
Understanding fundamental physical constraints from symmetry

primitives.name: CPT Symmetry
primitives.formalStatement: The CPT theorem states that any Lorentz-invariant local quantum field theory is invariant under the combined operation of charge conjugation (C), parity reversal (P), and time reversal (T). CPT symmetry implies particle-antiparticle mass equality and equal total decay rates.
primitives.computationalForm: Apply C (swap particles and antiparticles), P (mirror spatial coordinates), T (reverse time direction) simultaneously. The CPT-transformed S-matrix equals the original: S_{CPT} = S. Verify: m_particle = m_antiparticle, Gamma_total(particle) = Gamma_total(antiparticle).

dependencies.description: CPT theorem applies to Lagrangian field theories
CPT symmetry constrains what conservation laws can hold

compositionRules.yields: Constraints on CP violation and matter-antimatter asymmetry
compositionRules.example: CP violation in the weak interaction (observed in kaon and B-meson decays) must be compensated by T violation to preserve CPT

applicabilityPatterns: Establishing the most fundamental symmetry of quantum field theory
Constraining theories of matter-antimatter asymmetry
Testing fundamental physics by looking for CPT violations

primitives.name: String Action
primitives.formalStatement: The Nambu-Goto action S_NG = -T integral d^2 sigma sqrt(-det(h_{alpha beta})) describes a relativistic string propagating through spacetime, where h_{alpha beta} = partial_alpha X^mu partial_beta X_mu is the induced metric on the worldsheet and T = 1/(2*pi*alpha') is the string tension.

dependencies.description: The string action is a worldsheet area integral
String tension has dimensions of energy/length
String action generalizes the point particle action to extended objects

compositionRules.yields: Consistent string theory requiring 10 or 26 spacetime dimensions
compositionRules.example: Quantizing the bosonic string gives a conformal anomaly unless d = 26; the superstring requires d = 10

applicabilityPatterns: Describing fundamental objects beyond point particles
Building quantum theories of gravity
Exploring the structure of spacetime at the Planck scale

primitives.name: Extra Dimensions and Compactification
primitives.formalStatement: String theory requires extra spatial dimensions (6 for superstrings, 22 for bosonic strings) beyond the 3+1 spacetime dimensions we observe. Compactification curls extra dimensions into small manifolds: M^{10} = M^{3,1} x K^6 where K^6 is a compact Calabi-Yau manifold.

dependencies.description: Extra dimensions arise from string quantization consistency
Compactification involves topological properties of the extra dimensions

compositionRules.yields: Classification of compactification geometries and their physical consequences
conditions: Topology of compact manifold is specified
compositionRules.example: Compactifying on a 6-torus T^6 preserves all supersymmetries; Calabi-Yau compactification breaks to N=1 SUSY

applicabilityPatterns: Understanding why string theory predicts more than 4 spacetime dimensions
Connecting string theory to observable 4D physics
Exploring the landscape of possible low-energy effective theories

primitives.name: T-Duality
primitives.formalStatement: T-duality is an exact equivalence between string theory on a circle of radius R and string theory on a circle of radius alpha'/R (where alpha' = l_s^2 is the string length squared). Momentum modes on one side map to winding modes on the other: n <-> w, R <-> alpha'/R.
primitives.computationalForm: Under T-duality: X_L^25 -> X_L^25, X_R^25 -> -X_R^25 (flip right-movers). Momentum quantum number n <-> winding number w. String spectrum is invariant: M^2 = (n/R)^2 + (wR/alpha')^2 + ... is symmetric under R <-> alpha'/R with n <-> w.

dependencies.description: T-duality is a symmetry of the string spectrum
T-duality relates different compactification radii

compositionRules.yields: Web of string theory dualities connecting all five superstring theories
conditions: Both dualities applied to different compactification parameters
compositionRules.example: Type IIA on circle of radius R is T-dual to Type IIB on radius alpha'/R

applicabilityPatterns: Discovering equivalences between apparently different string theories
Understanding the minimum length scale in string theory
Exploring non-perturbative string theory

primitives.name: S-Duality
primitives.formalStatement: S-duality is an equivalence between a string theory at coupling g_s and the same (or different) theory at coupling 1/g_s. It maps the perturbative regime of one theory to the non-perturbative regime of another: weak coupling <-> strong coupling.

dependencies.description: S-duality relates different coupling regimes of string theory
S-duality complements T-duality in the duality web

compositionRules.yields: Complete duality network relating all five consistent superstring theories
conditions: Combined T and S dualities considered
compositionRules.example: Type I string theory at strong coupling is S-dual to SO(32) heterotic string theory at weak coupling

applicabilityPatterns: Understanding non-perturbative string theory through weak-coupling calculations
Connecting different string theories as limits of a single framework
Computing strong-coupling results using dual weak-coupling descriptions

primitives.name: AdS/CFT Correspondence
primitives.formalStatement: The AdS/CFT correspondence (Maldacena conjecture) states that string theory on AdS_{d+1} x S^{p} is equivalent to a conformal field theory on the d-dimensional boundary. The most studied case: Type IIB string theory on AdS_5 x S^5 is dual to N=4 Super Yang-Mills in 4D.
primitives.computationalForm: Correlation functions in the CFT are computed from string theory via: <O_1(x_1)...O_n(x_n)>_CFT = Z_string[phi_i -> phi_i^0 on boundary]. In the large N limit, string theory reduces to classical gravity, and CFT correlators are computed from bulk geodesics and gravitational actions.

dependencies.description: AdS/CFT is a duality of string theory
Both sides of the duality have quantum mechanical Hilbert spaces that are identified

compositionRules.yields: Categorical formulation of holographic duality as a functor between theories
conditions: Categorical language is used for both sides of the duality
compositionRules.example: The duality maps bulk operators to boundary operators, preserving the algebraic structure: a functor from bulk algebra to boundary algebra

applicabilityPatterns: Computing strongly coupled gauge theory observables using gravity
Understanding quantum gravity through field theory
Studying quantum information in gravitational systems

primitives.name: Holographic Principle
primitives.formalStatement: The holographic principle states that the information content of a region of space is bounded by the area of its boundary, not its volume: S <= A/(4 l_P^2), where l_P is the Planck length. A (d+1)-dimensional gravitational theory is encoded by a d-dimensional boundary theory.
primitives.computationalForm: Compute the maximum entropy in a region: S_max = A/(4 G_N) in natural units. For a black hole, this bound is saturated: S_BH = A/(4 l_P^2). The Bekenstein bound for general systems: S <= 2*pi*R*E/(hbar*c).

dependencies.description: AdS/CFT is the most concrete realization of holography
Information-theoretic entropy quantifies the holographic bound

compositionRules.yields: Holographic entropy bounds constraining information storage in gravitational systems
compositionRules.example: A black hole of mass M has entropy S = 4*pi*G*M^2/(hbar*c), the maximum entropy for that radius

applicabilityPatterns: Bounding information content of physical systems
Understanding the deep relationship between spacetime geometry and quantum information
Resolving the black hole information paradox

primitives.name: M-Theory
primitives.formalStatement: M-theory is the conjectured 11-dimensional theory that unifies all five consistent superstring theories as different perturbative limits. Its low-energy limit is 11D supergravity. M-theory contains membranes (M2-branes) and five-branes (M5-branes) as fundamental objects.

dependencies.description: T-duality connects string theories that are M-theory limits
S-duality connects perturbative and non-perturbative limits
M-theory generalizes string theory to include membranes

compositionRules.yields: Derivation of all five superstring theories as M-theory compactifications
compositionRules.example: M-theory on S^1 gives Type IIA; M-theory on S^1/Z_2 gives heterotic E_8 x E_8

applicabilityPatterns: Seeking the unique underlying theory of quantum gravity
Understanding non-perturbative string theory
Exploring the fundamental structure of spacetime

primitives.name: Supersymmetry
primitives.formalStatement: Supersymmetry (SUSY) is a symmetry relating bosons and fermions: the supercharge Q transforms bosons into fermions and vice versa, Q|boson> = |fermion>, Q|fermion> = |boson>. The SUSY algebra: {Q_alpha, Q_bar_beta} = 2 sigma^mu_{alpha beta} P_mu.

dependencies.description: SUSY extends the Poincare group with fermionic generators
SUSY constrains the form of the Lagrangian

compositionRules.yields: Supersymmetric gauge theories with improved UV behavior
conditions: SUSY is combined with gauge invariance
compositionRules.example: N=4 Super Yang-Mills is the most symmetric 4D gauge theory: conformal, finite to all loop orders

applicabilityPatterns: Addressing the hierarchy problem in particle physics
Constructing consistent quantum theories of gravity
Improving ultraviolet behavior of quantum field theories

primitives.name: Calabi-Yau Manifold
primitives.formalStatement: A Calabi-Yau manifold is a compact Kahler manifold with vanishing first Chern class (equivalently, admitting a Ricci-flat metric). In string theory, compactification on a Calabi-Yau 3-fold preserves N=1 supersymmetry in 4D. The topology (Hodge numbers h^{1,1}, h^{2,1}) determines the particle spectrum.

dependencies.description: Calabi-Yau manifolds are the compactification spaces
Topological invariants of the Calabi-Yau determine physical properties

compositionRules.yields: Specific 4D particle physics from string compactification
compositionRules.example: Compactification on a Calabi-Yau with h^{1,1}=3, h^{2,1}=243 gives a specific 4D effective theory with determined gauge groups and matter content

applicabilityPatterns: Connecting string theory to 4D particle physics
Classifying possible low-energy effective theories from string theory
Understanding how geometry determines physics

primitives.name: String Landscape
primitives.formalStatement: The string landscape is the vast collection of possible vacuum states (estimated at 10^500 or more) arising from different compactification choices, flux configurations, and brane arrangements in string theory. Each vacuum corresponds to a different low-energy effective theory with different particle content and coupling constants.

dependencies.description: Different Calabi-Yau topologies populate the landscape
Different compactifications give different vacua

compositionRules.yields: Statistical analysis of the string landscape (vacuum statistics)
conditions: Measure on the landscape is defined
compositionRules.example: Douglas's vacuum statistics: the number of flux vacua with cosmological constant Lambda scales as N(Lambda) ~ Lambda^{b_4/2} where b_4 is the fourth Betti number

applicabilityPatterns: Understanding the predictive limitations of string theory
Exploring the anthropic landscape and vacuum selection
Analyzing the space of consistent quantum gravity theories

primitives.name: Black Hole Entropy
primitives.formalStatement: The Bekenstein-Hawking entropy of a black hole is S_BH = A/(4 G_N) = A/(4 l_P^2), where A is the area of the event horizon. String theory provides a microscopic derivation via D-brane state counting, exactly matching S_BH for extremal and near-extremal black holes (Strominger-Vafa).
primitives.computationalForm: For a Schwarzschild black hole of mass M: A = 16*pi*G^2*M^2/c^4, so S = 4*pi*G*M^2/(hbar*c). In string theory, count BPS microstates: S_micro = log(N_states). The Strominger-Vafa result: S_micro = 2*pi*sqrt(n_1*n_5*n_p) matches S_BH exactly.

dependencies.description: Microscopic entropy counting uses string theory
Entropy quantifies the information content / number of microstates
Black hole entropy is the original motivation for holography

compositionRules.yields: Microscopic information-theoretic interpretation of black hole thermodynamics
compositionRules.example: Strominger and Vafa counted D-brane microstates for a 5D extremal black hole and obtained S = A/(4G) exactly

applicabilityPatterns: Providing a microscopic foundation for black hole thermodynamics
Testing quantum gravity proposals through entropy matching
Connecting string theory to observable gravitational physics

primitives.name: Black Hole Information Paradox
primitives.formalStatement: The information paradox arises because black hole evaporation via Hawking radiation appears to destroy information (pure states evolve to mixed states), violating quantum mechanical unitarity. Resolution proposals include: information encoded in subtle correlations in Hawking radiation, complementarity, and the Page curve from replica wormholes.

dependencies.description: The information paradox involves the entropy of black holes
Holography suggests information is preserved on the boundary
Unitarity of quantum evolution is at stake

compositionRules.yields: Resolution via holographic encoding of information on the boundary
compositionRules.example: In AdS/CFT, bulk black hole formation and evaporation is dual to unitary boundary CFT evolution, so information is preserved

applicabilityPatterns: Understanding the fundamental nature of information in physics
Probing the consistency of quantum gravity
Motivating new approaches to quantum gravity

primitives.name: Emergent Spacetime
primitives.formalStatement: In approaches to quantum gravity, spacetime itself may be an emergent phenomenon rather than fundamental. Entanglement structure of the boundary CFT in AdS/CFT determines bulk spacetime geometry via the RT formula: S(A) = Area(gamma_A)/(4 G_N), where gamma_A is the minimal surface homologous to boundary region A.

dependencies.description: Emergent spacetime is realized concretely in AdS/CFT
Holography implies spacetime geometry encodes boundary information
Entanglement entropy drives spacetime emergence

compositionRules.yields: ER=EPR: entanglement as the fabric of spacetime connectivity
compositionRules.example: Maldacena and Susskind's ER=EPR conjecture: Einstein-Rosen bridges (wormholes) are Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen entanglement pairs

applicabilityPatterns: Understanding the fundamental nature of spacetime
Connecting quantum information to gravitational geometry
Exploring how classical spacetime arises from quantum degrees of freedom

primitives.name: Moduli Space
primitives.formalStatement: The moduli space of a string compactification is the space of continuous parameters (moduli) that characterize the vacuum, including the shape and size of the compact manifold. Moduli are massless scalar fields in the effective theory that must be stabilized to give a physically viable vacuum.

dependencies.description: Calabi-Yau moduli parametrize the compactification geometry
Moduli arise from the freedom in compactification
Moduli space has a natural topological and geometric structure

compositionRules.yields: Discretuum of flux vacua within the continuous moduli space
compositionRules.example: In Type IIB on a Calabi-Yau with h^{2,1} complex structure moduli, flux choices discretize the continuous moduli space into O(10^{500}) vacua

applicabilityPatterns: Classifying the degrees of freedom in string compactifications
Understanding vacuum degeneracy in string theory
Studying the geometry of solution spaces in mathematical physics

primitives.name: Gauge Bosons
primitives.formalStatement: Gauge bosons are the force-carrying particles that mediate fundamental interactions. They arise as the gauge fields required by local gauge invariance: photon (gamma, U(1) EM), W+/-, Z^0 (SU(2)_L x U(1)_Y electroweak), and 8 gluons (SU(3)_C strong). All are spin-1 vector bosons.
primitives.computationalForm: The number of gauge bosons equals the dimension of the gauge group's Lie algebra: U(1) -> 1 photon, SU(2) -> 3 (W+, W-, Z after mixing), SU(3) -> 8 gluons. Total: 12 gauge bosons in the Standard Model (before symmetry breaking: 4 electroweak + 8 strong).

dependencies.description: Gauge bosons arise from the gauge principle
Number and properties of gauge bosons determined by the gauge group

compositionRules.yields: Massive W and Z bosons plus massless photon
compositionRules.example: Before symmetry breaking: 4 massless electroweak gauge bosons. After: 3 massive (W+, W-, Z) + 1 massless (photon)

applicabilityPatterns: Identifying force carriers in the Standard Model
Computing interaction vertices for scattering processes
Understanding the particle content required by gauge invariance
